Pritilata Wadedar and Kalpana Dutta

[Azaadi](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/azaadi/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Pritilata Wadedar and Kalpana Dutta

### The Heroines of Chittagong

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

May 05, 2025

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

Share

The Chittagong Armoury Raid masterminded by [Master Da Surya Sen](https://sadashree.substack.com/p/master-da-surya-sen), had many heroes, it also had two heroines, or rather two tigresses- Pritilata Wadedar and Kalpana Dutta.

Pritilata Wadedar infiltrated the Pahartali European Club, disguised as a punjabi man, leading a revolutionary team in a daring attack against colonial privilege. Her last act was swallowing cyanide to evade capture—an agonizing but resolute farewell.

Kalpana Dutta, fierce and fearless, took part in direct armed resistance alongside her comrades. She was later arrested and sentenced to transportation for life, but her legacy remained unshaken. Her memoirs and later political engagement helped carry forward the voice of resistance she once roared through a rifle.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BLb7!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4be93c04-4047-4242-9204-2bc96966e587_189x267.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BLb7!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4be93c04-4047-4242-9204-2bc96966e587_189x267.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BAfb!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F78034c24-8dce-426f-8c1c-2063f79962e7_213x236.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BAfb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F78034c24-8dce-426f-8c1c-2063f79962e7_213x236.jpeg)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!al5J!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7cda60b0-6736-4911-9709-c4bdb434c6fe_220x315.jpeg)

![pw](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!al5J!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F7cda60b0-6736-4911-9709-c4bdb434c6fe_220x315.jpeg "pw")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Patiya and Sripur—humble places from which two incandescent souls emerged, lighting up the Chittagong resistance like twin flames against colonial darkness. That busy route from Chittagong to Cox’s Bazar, now traversed by tourists and trade, once witnessed the quiet passage of girls destined to shake empires.

The mundane backdrop—Jagabandhu Wadedar at the municipality, Prathibamayi raising a daughter with a fire within—only makes Pritilata’s story more extraordinary. It's in those small, sleepy towns, hidden behind ordinary lives, that revolutions find their seed.

Incidentally Wadedar was not their surname, but a title given by the British. The 2nd of 6 children, others being Madhusudhan( her elder brother), Kanakalata, Shantilata, Ashalata and Santosh, this young girl nicknamed as Rani, would forever be enshrined in history of India’s revolutionary freedom struggle, due to an attack on the Pahartali European Club in Chittagong.

Dr. Khastagir’s Government Girls School wasn’t just a building—it was a crucible of awakening. For a girl like Pritilata, growing up amid siblings and steered by an education-focused father, it became the gateway to a life driven by purpose. Imagine the contrast: colonial authorities stamping “Wadedar” as a bureaucratic label, and yet this same girl tearing through their legacy with moral ferocity.

Her attack on the Pahartali European Club wasn’t just a tactical blow—it was symbolic, targeted at the very nerve center of exclusion. "Dogs and Indians not allowed," the sign outside sneered. And Pritilata answered with smoke, fire, and self-sacrifice,

*We had no clear idea in our school days about our future. Then the Rani of Jhansi fired our imagination with her example. Sometimes we used to think of ourselves as fearless- Kalpana Datta*

Bethune College, Kolkata, a cradle of awakening. In the same classrooms where quiet minds gathered, Usha Di transformed chalk and stories into flint and flame. The image of Rani Lakshmibai charging into battle—draped in valor—wasn’t just a memory; it was ammunition. For Pritilata, already steeped in nationalist fervor, it affirmed her resolve. For Kalpana, it unraveled the inherited loyalty to Empire and stitched a new allegiance—one forged by camaraderie, courage, and conscience.

Chhatri Sangha was less an organization and more a crucible. Kamla Dasgupta’s stewardship turned it into a sisterhood of resistance—where literature met lathi training, and debates on nationalism shared space with drills on physical combat. That image of young women from Victoria, Bethune, Scottish Church, Diocesan gathering not for tea, but for tactical rigor and ideological solidarity, flips every colonial stereotype on its head.

Pritilata's journey through Eden Mohila College and Dipali Sangha adds another beautiful contour. Leela Nag, as the first woman admitted to Dhaka University, was more than a role model—she was a torchbearer of feminine intellect and defiance. Under her guidance, Dipali Sangha must have felt like the incubator of a storm—with civic engagement paving the way to revolutionary awakening.

Under Leela Nag’s fierce guidance at Dipali Sangha, swordplay wasn’t mere theatrics—it was a political act. Every drill she mastered, every stance she held, was a defiance of Victorian fragility foisted upon Indian women. The arrest of Master Da Surya Sen, meanwhile, was no longer news—it was a wound, a call to arms. And as she leafed through texts on revolutionaries, history itself became her combat training.

Her move to Kolkata and immersion in Chhatri Sangha—a space led by Kamala Dasgupta—felt like stepping into the heart of resistance. These weren’t just cells of activity, but ideological greenhouses, where nationalism matured through stories, study circles, and solidarity.

Graduating with distinction in Philosophy was no small feat, especially while living a double life—one of ideas, and one of insurgency. The withholding of her degree alongside Bina Das, another firebrand who famously attempted to assassinate Governor Stanley Jackson, underscored the colonial administration’s fear of educated, radical women.

It did not deter her resolve as she transitioned from student to Headmistress at Nandankanan Aparna Charan Girls’ School, where chalk in hand, ideologically radiant, she guided the young minds in a city simmering with revolutionary energy. Her presence alone turned that school into a quiet citadel of resistance.

Pritilata was swept away by the revolutionary fervour, and felt it was time for women also to join the struggle. She didn’t seek symbolic inclusion. She demanded full participation—with rifles, strategy, sacrifice. That conviction, shaped in study circles and forged through the flame of action, made her one of India’s earliest and fiercest voices for feminist resistance.

One of her brothers was already active with the anti British group headed by Surya Sen, and he introduced her to Master Da.

She met Surya Sen and his associate Nirmal Sen at their Dhalaghat camp in 1932, and Master Da was impressed by her. Master Da saw in her not just conviction but practical advantage. It’s telling that amidst the testosterone of the times, he looked past gender norms and tapped into her potential—not merely for symbolic inclusion, but tactical value. The idea that women like Pritilata could move beneath the radar of British suspicion shows just how nuanced revolutionary planning was becoming.

That initial role of messenger might seem peripheral, but in movements of such precision, it’s the silent runners who carry the pulse. And one particular assignment left its mark—this feels like the emotional turning point, where she doesn’t just support revolution, she begins to breathe it.

In one of their previous assignments, in 1931, Surya Sen planned to kill the then Inspector General of Chittagong, Craig and assigned Ramakrishna Biswas, Kalipada Chakravarthy for this.However by mistake they ended up shooting dead the SP of Chandpur, Tarini Mukherjee and were arrested for this

Ramakrishna Biswas, sentenced to death, faced not just judicial retribution but emotional isolation. That his family, shackled by poverty, couldn't make the journey to Alipore Jail speaks volumes about the silent agonies that roared beneath the headlines. The act had gone awry, but its consequence sent ripples through the revolutionary ranks—deepening resolve, yes, but also foreshadowing sacrifice.

Pritilata who was in Kolkata at that time, was asked to meet Biswas. She managed to convince the jailor that she was a distant relative of Biswas, and met him around 40 times, and often had long conversations with him.

Forty visits. That’s not espionage—it’s devotion. Her ability to convince the jailor speaks of her calm grit, and each conversation must have been steeped in reflection, grief, fire. If indeed mutual affection blossomed, it only deepens the tragedy, because she wasn’t just watching a comrade walk to the gallows—she was watching someone she may have loved.

And yet, she didn’t unravel. Her sorrow galvanized her. Biswas’s impending execution became her baptism of fire, a point where personal pain became political fuel. This is the kind of moment where historical figures shed abstraction and become heartbreakingly real.

Pritilata soon took part in many raids, on Telephone and Telegraph offices, and played a key role in the Jalalabad attack, where her responsibility was to supply the explosives.

From messenger to munitions supplier, Pritilata’s transformation was complete. Her role in the Jalalabad attack, supplying explosives, proves she wasn’t a symbolic participant—she was central to operations, wielding both intellect and danger with striking resolve. These weren’t minor strikes. Sabotaging Telephone and Telegraph offices was a direct assault on colonial coordination—like severing the arteries of Empire.

The escape at Dhalaghat, surviving a police onslaught alongside Master Da while losing Nirmal Sen, must have seared her soul. That moment holds cinematic power—a fugitive glancing back as dust and death settle, her comrade gone, her mission sharpened. Becoming one of the most wanted revolutionaries marks not just notoriety but recognition. Even the British now acknowledged her threat.

When Master Da advised her to go underground, it was no longer about secrecy—it was preservation of fire. And she didn’t vanish alone. Kalpana Dutta, her sister in struggle, walked into the shadows with her. Together, they became thunderclouds on the colonial horizon.

Kalpana Dutta had joined the Indian Republican Army, after the Chittagong Armory Raid of 1930, where she would meet Master Da whom she described as *He was a smallish short man, very reserved. Nobody would guess that this man was the daring ‘King of Chittagong.* Impressed by his character and demeanour, she soon learnt guerilla warfare under him and became an active member of the resistance group.

Kalpana Dutta’s training with Mani Dutt, mastering the art of underwater concealment, underscores the lengths to which revolutionaries went. It wasn’t simply about guns—it was about outthinking and outlasting a system built to crush them. That she could remain underwater for hours using breath control evokes cinematic tension—and immense discipline.

Anant Singh, taught her the volatile craft of gun cotton—the very heartbeat of improvised explosives. And she didn’t just dabble. She excelled. Her capacity to absorb technique and fuse it with purpose turned her into a formidable insurgent whose mind was as dangerous as her munitions.

In a way both Kalpana and Pritilata, broke the “unsaid rule” that women were not fit enough to join the guerilla movement and male revolutionaries should steer clear of women.

It was their ability that gave Master Da enough confidence to entrust them with another daring mission, the raid on the Pahartali club in Chittagong, one of the exclusively for Europeans club, which had a signboard saying “Dogs and Indians not allowed”.

However with Kalpana Dutta being arrested, Master Da choose Pritilata Wadedar for the mission, and Kotawali Sea Side was where she planned the attack, as also the necessary arms training.

At Kotwali Sea Side, the conspirators trained not just with weapons, but with resolve. Cyanide capsules tucked into their clothing—a chilling reminder that this was an all-or-nothing operation. Each disguise, whether dhoti or lungi, wasn’t just cover—it was cultural camouflage. Pritilata as a Punjabi male adds a layer of audacity; she weaponized identity itself.

The date—23rd September 1932—should be etched like a thunderclap in history. That night, when colonials sipped privilege behind racist signage, a quiet band of revolutionaries burst in, not just with bombs, but with a message: that freedom wouldn’t wait politely outside.

That night, *23rd September 1932*, the Pahartali European Club became the stage where colonial arrogance was confronted by revolutionary fire. With thirty-nine others inside, all targets of symbolic justice, the plan unfolded with surgical precision—three flanks striking at 10:45 PM, the hour when leisure met reckoning. The resistance wasn’t indiscriminate—it was calibrated to disrupt, provoke, and demand notice.

When revolvers fired back, chaos reigned. But it’s Pritilata’s fate that carves itself deepest into memory: wounded, cornered, and still unbroken. Her cyanide capsule was not escape—it was chosen sacrifice. Her refusal to be paraded as a captured insurgent turned her into a symbol no trial or punishment could dismantle.

The police later found her dead body , and she was in possession of some leaflets, bullets, a photograph of Ramakrishna Biswas and the draft of their attack plan. She was just 21 when she died, however her tale would be an inspiration for many revolutionaries in Bengal and also in India, a true heroine, and a great daughter of the soil.

Her friend's suicide greatly affected Kalpana Dutta, who was now at home under police observation. While under surveillance, she remained a shadow that refused to fade. Her escape after Master Da’s arrest in February 1933 shows she still believed resistance must survive even as its leaders fell. That act alone, slipping away from the tightening grip of colonial forces, was a quiet rebellion.

Then came the Second Supplementary Trial of the Chittagong Armory Raid—a somber echo of 1930’s uprising. Her arrest and imprisonment in Hijli Jail, alongside other political prisoners, marks her final descent into history’s crucible. Hijli wasn’t just confinement—it was containment of voices too fierce to ignore.

It was here she met Bina Das, who was arrested for the attempted murder of Bengal’s Governor at Kolkata University. It was at this time she was attracted towards Communism and the works of Marx and Lenin.

Released in 1939, she graduated from Kolkata University in 1940 and joined the Communist Party of India. During the 1943 Bengal famine she served as a relief worker, as well as during Partition, when refugees flooded Kolkata. She also married P.C.Joshi then General Secretary of Communist Party.

But her most well known work would be “Chattagram Astragara Akramanakaridera Smriti Katha”, an autobiographical work, that describes her life as well as the Chittagong Armory Raid. The book was later translated into English as Chittagong Armoury Raiders: Reminiscences by Arun Bose & Nikhil Chakraborty in 1945.

Her 1946 electoral run as a Communist Party candidate from Chittagong, though unsuccessful, marked her transition from underground insurgent to public political voice. That she later joined the Indian Statistical Institute and worked there until retirement reflects a quieter phase of service—one rooted in intellect and nation-building.

Her passing on February 8, 1995, closed a chapter, but her legacy didn’t fade—it multiplied. Her son Chand Joshi, through *Bhindranwale: Myth and Reality*, tackled another volatile chapter of Indian history. And her daughter-in-law Manini Chatterjee, with *Do and Die: The Chittagong Uprising 1930–34*, gave the uprising its most vivid, human retelling. That book, meticulously researched and emotionally resonant, ensured that Master Da, Pritilata, Kalpana, and their comrades would never be footnotes again.

And at the heart of it all remains Kalpana’s own memoir—*Chattagram Astragara Akramanakaridera Smriti Katha*—a revolutionary’s remembrance, a daughter of the soil writing history with the ink of lived experience.
